title: Die Bremer Stadtmusikanten
source: Brüder Grimm, Kinder- und Hausmärchen, Band 1, 7. Auflage (Ausgabe letzter Hand), Dieterich, Göttingen 1857, KHM 27. Text from Wikisource („Die Bremer Stadtmusikanten (1857)“, twice proofread against the original). Fresh translation made directly from the German; 1857 spelling preserved exactly.
about: The twenty-seventh tale (KHM 27). Four worn-out animals — a donkey, dog, cat and cock, all cast off once past their usefulness — set out for Bremen to become town musicians, but on the way they frighten a band of robbers out of a snug house and settle there instead. 1857 spelling is kept, so you will meet older forms such as gieng, daß, todt and musicieren.

sentences.t: Es hatte ein Mann einen Esel, der schon lange Jahre die Säcke unverdrossen zur Mühle getragen hatte, dessen Kräfte aber nun zu Ende giengen, so daß er zur Arbeit immer untauglicher ward.
sentences.l: A man had a donkey, who for many long years had carried the sacks untiringly to the mill, but whose strengths were now coming to an end, so that he grew ever more unfit for the work.
sentences.i: There was once a man who had a donkey that had for long years carried the sacks to the mill without complaint, but whose strength was now failing, so that he grew more and more unfit for work.
sentences.n: “Es hatte ein Mann einen Esel” fronts the verb (literally ‘there had a man a donkey’) — a common fairy-tale opening. “dessen Kräfte” is the genitive relative ‘whose strengths’. “ward” is the archaic preterite of werden (modern wurde), which you meet constantly in the 1857 text.

sentences.t: Da dachte der Herr daran, ihn aus dem Futter zu schaffen, aber der Esel merkte daß kein guter Wind wehte, lief fort und machte sich auf den Weg nach Bremen: dort, meinte er, könnte er ja Stadtmusikant werden.
sentences.l: Then the master thought of getting him out of the fodder, but the donkey noticed that no good wind was blowing, ran off and set out on the way to Bremen: there, he thought, he could surely become a town-musician.
sentences.i: Then the master began thinking of doing away with him, but the donkey saw which way the wind was blowing, ran off and took the road to Bremen: there, he thought, he might well become a town musician.
sentences.n: “ihn aus dem Futter zu schaffen” (literally ‘to get him out of the fodder’) is a grim euphemism for getting rid of the animal. “daß kein guter Wind wehte” — that no good wind was blowing — is an idiom for sensing danger.

sentences.t: Als er ein Weilchen fortgegangen war, fand er einen Jagdhund auf dem Wege liegen, der jappte wie einer, der sich müde gelaufen hat.
sentences.l: When he had gone on a little while, he found a hunting-dog lying on the way, who panted like one who has run himself tired.
sentences.i: When he had gone on a little way, he found a hound lying in the road, panting like one who has run himself weary.
sentences.n: “fand er ... liegen” — ‘found him lying’ — an infinitive of perception. “jappen” is a dialectal word for panting/gasping; “sich müde laufen” = to tire oneself out by running (reflexive).

sentences.t: „Nun, was jappst du so, Packan?“ fragte der Esel.
sentences.l: “Now, why do you pant so, Packan?” asked the donkey.
sentences.n: Packan is a common old name for a big dog — literally ‘seize-hold’ (pack an!).

sentences.t: „Ach,“ sagte der Hund, „weil ich alt bin und jeden Tag schwächer werde, auch auf der Jagd nicht mehr fort kann, hat mich mein Herr wollen todt schlagen, da hab ich Reißaus genommen; aber womit soll ich nun mein Brot verdienen?“
sentences.l: “Oh,” said the dog, “because I am old and grow weaker every day, and can no longer keep up on the hunt, my master wanted to strike me dead, so I took to my heels; but with what shall I now earn my bread?”
sentences.i: “Ah,” said the dog, “because I am old and grow weaker by the day, and can no longer keep up in the hunt, my master meant to kill me, so I took to my heels; but how am I now to earn my bread?”
sentences.n: “hat mich ... wollen todt schlagen” uses the double-infinitive with wollen (not the past participle gewollt). “todt schlagen” is the 1857 spelling of totschlagen (to beat to death). “Reißaus nehmen” = to bolt, take to one’s heels.

sentences.t: „Weißt du was,“ sprach der Esel, „ich gehe nach Bremen und werde dort Stadtmusikant, geh mit und laß dich auch bei der Musik annehmen. Ich spiele die Laute, und du schlägst die Pauken.“
sentences.l: “You know what,” said the donkey, “I am going to Bremen and shall become a town-musician there; come along and let yourself be taken on with the music too. I play the lute, and you beat the kettledrums.”
sentences.i: “I’ll tell you what,” said the donkey, “I am off to Bremen to become a town musician; come along and get yourself engaged in the music too. I shall play the lute, and you shall beat the drums.”
sentences.n: “laß dich ... annehmen” — let yourself be taken on/engaged; annehmen here means to hire or engage. Note the vivid present tense used for the future (“ich gehe”, “ich spiele”).

sentences.t: Der Hund wars zufrieden, und sie giengen weiter.
sentences.l: The dog was content with it, and they went on further.
sentences.i: The dog was well pleased, and on they went.
sentences.n: “wars” = war es (‘was it’) — a clitic contraction, very common in the tales.

sentences.t: Es dauerte nicht lange, so saß da eine Katze an dem Weg und machte ein Gesicht wie drei Tage Regenwetter.
sentences.l: It did not last long, and there sat a cat by the way and made a face like three days of rainy weather.
sentences.i: It was not long before there sat a cat by the roadside, making a face like three days of rain.
sentences.n: “ein Gesicht wie drei Tage Regenwetter machen” — to pull a face as gloomy as three days of rain — a still-current idiom.

sentences.t: „Nun, was ist dir in die Quere gekommen, alter Bartputzer?“ sprach der Esel.
sentences.l: “Now, what has come across your way, old beard-cleaner?” said the donkey.
sentences.i: “Now, what has crossed your path, old whisker-washer?” said the donkey.
sentences.n: “in die Quere kommen” = to get in the way / cross one’s path. “Bartputzer” (beard-cleaner) is a jesting nickname for the cat, always licking its whiskers.

sentences.t: „Wer kann da lustig sein, wenns einem an den Kragen geht,“ antwortete die Katze, „weil ich nun zu Jahren komme, meine Zähne stumpf werden, und ich lieber hinter dem Ofen sitze und spinne, als nach Mäusen herum jage, hat mich meine Frau ersäufen wollen; ich habe mich zwar noch fortgemacht, aber nun ist guter Rath theuer: wo soll ich hin?“
sentences.l: “Who can be merry when it goes for one’s collar,” answered the cat, “because I am now getting on in years, my teeth grow blunt, and I would rather sit behind the stove and spin than chase around after mice, my mistress wanted to drown me; I did indeed still make off, but now good counsel is dear: where shall I go?”
sentences.i: “Who can be merry when his life is at stake,” answered the cat, “now that I am getting on in years, my teeth are growing blunt, and I would sooner sit behind the stove and purr than go chasing after mice, my mistress wanted to drown me; I did get away in time, but now I am at my wits’ end: where am I to go?”
sentences.n: Two idioms: “einem an den Kragen gehen” (to go for someone’s collar) = one’s life is at stake; “guter Rath ist theuer” (good counsel is dear/costly) = to be at a loss what to do. “zu Jahren kommen” = to get on in years. “Rath/theuer” keep their 1857 -th spelling.

sentences.t: „Geh mit uns nach Bremen, du verstehst dich doch auf die Nachtmusik, da kannst du ein Stadtmusikant werden.“
sentences.l: “Come with us to Bremen; you do understand yourself upon the night-music, so you can become a town-musician.”
sentences.i: “Come with us to Bremen; you are surely a master of the nightly serenade, so you can become a town musician.”
sentences.n: “sich verstehen auf (etwas)” = to be skilled at something. “Nachtmusik” (night music) is a sly dig at the cat’s yowling. “doch” softens it to ‘surely, after all’.

sentences.t: Die Katze hielt das für gut und gieng mit.
sentences.l: The cat held that for good and went along.
sentences.i: The cat thought this good and went along.
sentences.n: “etwas für gut halten” = to consider something good. “gieng” is the 1857 spelling of ging.

sentences.t: Darauf kamen die drei Landesflüchtigen an einem Hof vorbei, da saß auf dem Thor der Haushahn und schrie aus Leibeskräften.
sentences.l: Thereupon the three fugitives came past a farmyard, where on the gate sat the house-cock and screamed with all his bodily might.
sentences.i: After that the three fugitives passed by a farmyard, where the farmyard cock sat upon the gate and crowed with all his might.
sentences.n: “Landesflüchtige” (nominalized adjective) = fugitives, those fleeing the land. “Thor” is the 1857 spelling of Tor (gate). “aus Leibeskräften” = with all one’s bodily strength.

sentences.t: „Du schreist einem durch Mark und Bein,“ sprach der Esel, „was hast du vor?“
sentences.l: “You scream through one’s marrow and bone,” said the donkey, “what are you up to?”
sentences.i: “Your screaming goes right through marrow and bone,” said the donkey, “what are you about?”
sentences.n: “durch Mark und Bein” (through marrow and bone) = piercingly, to the very core. “etwas vorhaben” (separable vor-) = to be planning/up to something.

sentences.t: „Da hab ich gut Wetter prophezeit,“ sprach der Hahn, „weil unserer lieben Frauen Tag ist, wo sie dem Christkindlein die Hemdchen gewaschen hat und sie trocknen will; aber weil Morgen zum Sonntag Gäste kommen, so hat die Hausfrau doch kein Erbarmen, und hat der Köchin gesagt sie wollte mich Morgen in der Suppe essen, und da soll ich mir heut Abend den Kopf abschneiden lassen.
sentences.l: “There I have prophesied good weather,” said the cock, “because it is Our Lady’s day, when she has washed the little shirts for the Christ-child and wants to dry them; but because guests are coming tomorrow for Sunday, the housewife has no mercy after all, and has told the cook she wanted to eat me tomorrow in the soup, and so this evening I am to have my head cut off.
sentences.i: “I have been prophesying good weather,” said the cock, “because it is Our Lady’s day, when she has washed the Christ-child’s little shirts and wants to dry them; but since guests are coming tomorrow for Sunday, the mistress has no mercy after all, and has told the cook she means to eat me in the soup tomorrow, and so tonight I am to have my head cut off.
sentences.n: “unserer lieben Frauen Tag” = Our Lady’s Day (a Marian feast); a fine day was expected so the Virgin could dry the washing. “sich den Kopf abschneiden lassen” — to have one’s head cut off — uses lassen for a caused action. Note the reported-speech subjunctive “sie wollte”.

sentences.t: Nun schrei ich aus vollem Hals, so lang ich noch kann.“
sentences.l: Now I scream at full throat, as long as I still can.”
sentences.i: So now I crow at the top of my voice, as long as I still can.”
sentences.n: “aus vollem Hals(e)” = at the top of one’s voice, literally ‘from a full throat’.

sentences.t: „Ei was, du Rothkopf,“ sagte der Esel, „zieh lieber mit uns fort, wir gehen nach Bremen, etwas besseres als den Tod findest du überall; du hast eine gute Stimme, und wenn wir zusammen musicieren, so muß es eine Art haben.“
sentences.l: “Eh what, you Redhead,” said the donkey, “rather move off with us; we are going to Bremen; something better than death you will find everywhere; you have a good voice, and when we make music together, then it must have a style to it.”
sentences.i: “Come now, Redhead,” said the donkey, “better come away with us; we are going to Bremen; you can find something better than death anywhere; you have a good voice, and when we make music together, it is bound to be something fine.”
sentences.n: “etwas besseres als den Tod findest du überall” is a proverbial line (‘anything is better than death’). “es muß eine Art haben” = it must have some style / turn out well. “musicieren” is the 1857 spelling of musizieren.

sentences.t: Der Hahn ließ sich den Vorschlag gefallen, und sie giengen alle viere zusammen fort.
sentences.l: The cock let the proposal please himself, and they went all four together off.
sentences.i: The cock let himself be won over to the plan, and all four of them went off together.
sentences.n: “sich etwas gefallen lassen” = to accept / put up with / agree to something. “alle viere” is an old inflected form of ‘all four’.

sentences.t: Sie konnten aber die Stadt Bremen in einem Tag nicht erreichen und kamen Abends in einen Wald, wo sie übernachten wollten.
sentences.l: But they could not reach the city of Bremen in one day, and came in the evening into a wood, where they wanted to spend the night.
sentences.i: But they could not reach the city of Bremen in a single day, and towards evening came into a wood, where they meant to pass the night.

sentences.t: Der Esel und der Hund legten sich unter einen großen Baum, die Katze und der Hahn machten sich in die Äste, der Hahn aber flog bis in die Spitze, wo es am sichersten für ihn war.
sentences.l: The donkey and the dog laid themselves down under a great tree, the cat and the cock betook themselves into the branches, but the cock flew right up into the top, where it was safest for him.
sentences.i: The donkey and the dog lay down under a great tree, the cat and the cock settled into the branches, but the cock flew right up to the very top, where he was safest.
sentences.n: “sich in die Äste machen” — to take oneself into the branches. “am sichersten” is the superlative of sicher (safe).

sentences.t: Ehe er einschlief, sah er sich noch einmal nach allen vier Winden um, da däuchte ihn er sähe in der Ferne ein Fünkchen brennen und rief seinen Gesellen zu es müßte nicht gar weit ein Haus sein, denn es scheine ein Licht.
sentences.l: Before he fell asleep, he looked around himself once more to all four winds; then it seemed to him he saw a little spark burning in the distance, and he called out to his companions that a house could not be very far off, for a light was shining.
sentences.i: Before he fell asleep, he looked round once more to all four winds, and it seemed to him that he saw a little spark burning in the distance, and he called out to his companions that a house could not be far off, for there was a light shining.
sentences.n: “däuchte ihn” = it seemed to him — an archaic impersonal (from dünken); you will meet it again in the tales. Three subjunctives of reported speech follow — “sähe”, “müßte”, “scheine” — marking what the cock claims to see.

sentences.t: Sprach der Esel „so müssen wir uns aufmachen und noch hingehen, denn hier ist die Herberge schlecht.“
sentences.l: Said the donkey, “then we must set out and go there, for here the lodging is poor.”
sentences.i: Said the donkey, “then we must be off and go on there, for the lodging here is poor.”
sentences.n: “sich aufmachen” (separable auf-) = to set out. The verb-first “Sprach der Esel” is a narrative inversion common in older German.

sentences.t: Der Hund meinte ein paar Knochen und etwas Fleisch dran, thäten ihm auch gut.
sentences.l: The dog reckoned a couple of bones with some meat on them would do him good too.
sentences.n: “thäten” is the subjunctive of thun/tun (would do) — reported thought — in its 1857 -th spelling. “dran” = daran (‘on them’).

sentences.t: Also machten sie sich auf den Weg nach der Gegend, wo das Licht war, und sahen es bald heller schimmern, und es ward immer größer, bis sie vor ein hell erleuchtetes Räuberhaus kamen.
sentences.l: So they set out on the way toward the region where the light was, and soon saw it shimmer brighter, and it grew ever bigger, until they came before a brightly lit robbers’ house.
sentences.i: So they set out toward the quarter where the light was, and soon saw it gleam brighter, and it grew larger and larger, until they came to a brightly lit robbers’ house.
sentences.n: “immer größer” — ever bigger; immer + comparative expresses steady increase. “ward” again = wurde.

sentences.t: Der Esel, als der größte, näherte sich dem Fenster und schaute hinein.
sentences.l: The donkey, as the biggest, approached the window and looked in.
sentences.i: The donkey, being the tallest, went up to the window and looked in.
sentences.n: “als der größte” = as/being the biggest. “sich nähern” takes the dative (dem Fenster).

sentences.t: „Was siehst du, Grauschimmel?“ fragte der Hahn.
sentences.l: “What do you see, Grey-horse?” asked the cock.
sentences.n: “Grauschimmel” (grey horse) is a joking name for the donkey.

sentences.t: „Was ich sehe?“ antwortete der Esel, „einen gedeckten Tisch mit schönem Essen und Trinken, und Räuber sitzen daran und lassens sich wohl sein.“
sentences.l: “What I see?” answered the donkey, “a laid table with fine food and drink, and robbers sit at it and let themselves be well.”
sentences.i: “What I see?” answered the donkey, “a table laid with good food and drink, and robbers sitting at it, making merry.”
sentences.n: “ein gedeckter Tisch” = a table laid (for a meal). “es sich wohl sein lassen” = to enjoy oneself, make merry; “lassens” = lassen es.

sentences.t: „Das wäre was für uns“ sprach der Hahn.
sentences.l: “That would be something for us,” said the cock.
sentences.n: “wäre” is subjunctive II expressing a wish/hypothetical; “was” = etwas (something).

sentences.t: „Ja, ja, ach, wären wir da!“ sagte der Esel.
sentences.l: “Yes, yes, ah, were we there!” said the donkey.
sentences.i: “Yes, yes, ah, if only we were there!” said the donkey.
sentences.n: “wären wir da” is a subjunctive II wish without wenn — ‘if only we were there’.

sentences.t: Da rathschlagten die Thiere wie sie es anfangen müßten, um die Räuber hinaus zu jagen und fanden endlich ein Mittel.
sentences.l: Then the animals took counsel how they must go about it, in order to drive the robbers out, and at last found a means.
sentences.i: Then the animals took counsel how they should set about it to drive the robbers out, and at last hit upon a plan.
sentences.n: “rathschlagen” (1857 spelling of ratschlagen) = to deliberate, take counsel. “etwas anfangen” here = to go about / set about a thing. “Thiere” keeps the old Th- spelling.

sentences.t: Der Esel mußte sich mit den Vorderfüßen auf das Fenster stellen, der Hund auf des Esels Rücken springen, die Katze auf den Hund klettern, und endlich flog der Hahn hinauf, und setzte sich der Katze auf den Kopf.
sentences.l: The donkey had to place himself with his forefeet on the window, the dog to spring onto the donkey’s back, the cat to climb onto the dog, and at last the cock flew up and set himself on the cat’s head.
sentences.i: The donkey had to plant his forefeet on the window, the dog to spring onto the donkey’s back, the cat to climb onto the dog, and at last the cock flew up and perched on the cat’s head.
sentences.n: “der Katze auf den Kopf” uses the dative of possession (‘onto the cat’s head’) — very common in German where English uses a possessive.

sentences.t: Wie das geschehen war, fiengen sie auf ein Zeichen insgesammt an ihre Musik zu machen: der Esel schrie, der Hund bellte, die Katze miaute und der Hahn krähte; dann stürzten sie durch das Fenster in die Stube hinein daß die Scheiben klirrten.
sentences.l: When that had happened, they all together, at a signal, began to make their music: the donkey brayed, the dog barked, the cat miaowed and the cock crowed; then they plunged through the window into the room so that the panes clattered.
sentences.i: When this had been done, at a signal they all struck up their music together: the donkey brayed, the dog barked, the cat miaowed and the cock crowed; then they burst through the window into the room, so that the panes rang.
sentences.n: “fiengen ... an” is the separable anfangen (to begin), here in the 1857 spelling fiengen (modern fingen). “insgesammt” = all together. “daß die Scheiben klirrten” — result clause, ‘so that the panes rattled’.

sentences.t: Die Räuber fuhren bei dem entsetzlichen Geschrei in die Höhe, meinten nicht anders als ein Gespenst käme herein und flohen in größter Furcht in den Wald hinaus.
sentences.l: The robbers started up at the dreadful screaming, thought nothing other than that a ghost was coming in, and fled in greatest fear out into the wood.
sentences.i: The robbers sprang up at the dreadful din, thought nothing but that a ghost was coming in, and fled in the greatest terror out into the wood.
sentences.n: “in die Höhe fahren” = to start up, jump up in fright. “meinten nicht anders als” = thought nothing other than; “käme” is reported-speech subjunctive.

sentences.t: Nun setzten sich die vier Gesellen an den Tisch, nahmen mit dem vorlieb, was übrig geblieben war, und aßen als wenn sie vier Wochen hungern sollten.
sentences.l: Now the four companions sat down at the table, made do with what was left over, and ate as if they were to go hungry for four weeks.
sentences.i: Now the four companions sat down at the table, contented themselves with what was left, and ate as though they must starve for four weeks.
sentences.n: “mit etwas vorlieb nehmen” = to make do / be content with something. “als wenn ... sollten” = as if they were bound to — a subjunctive of comparison.

sentences.t: Wie die vier Spielleute fertig waren, löschten sie das Licht aus und suchten sich eine Schlafstätte, jeder nach seiner Natur und Bequemlichkeit.
sentences.l: When the four minstrels were finished, they put out the light and each sought himself a sleeping-place, each according to his nature and comfort.
sentences.i: When the four minstrels had finished, they put out the light and each sought a resting-place, each after his own nature and liking.
sentences.n: “Spielleute” = minstrels, musicians (plural of Spielmann). “auslöschen” (separable aus-) = to put out; here löschten ... aus.

sentences.t: Der Esel legte sich auf den Mist, der Hund hinter die Thüre, die Katze auf den Herd bei die warme Asche, und der Hahn setzte sich auf den Hahnenbalken: und weil sie müde waren von ihrem langen Weg, schliefen sie auch bald ein.
sentences.l: The donkey laid himself on the dung-heap, the dog behind the door, the cat on the hearth by the warm ashes, and the cock set himself on the roost-beam: and because they were tired from their long journey, they soon fell asleep.
sentences.i: The donkey lay down on the dung-heap, the dog behind the door, the cat on the hearth by the warm ashes, and the cock perched on the roost-beam: and because they were weary from their long journey, they were soon fast asleep.
sentences.n: The verb “legte sich” carries over to the dog and cat by ellipsis. “Thüre” is the 1857 spelling of Türe. “einschlafen” (separable ein-) = to fall asleep.

sentences.t: Als Mitternacht vorbei war, und die Räuber von weitem sahen daß kein Licht mehr im Haus brannte, auch alles ruhig schien, sprach der Hauptmann „wir hätten uns doch nicht sollen ins Bockshorn jagen lassen,“ und hieß einen hingehen und das Haus untersuchen.
sentences.l: When midnight was past, and the robbers saw from afar that no light was burning in the house any more, and all seemed quiet too, the captain said, “we should not have let ourselves be scared into a corner after all,” and bade one of them go over and search the house.
sentences.i: When midnight was past, and the robbers saw from a distance that no light was burning in the house any longer, and that all seemed quiet, the captain said, “we ought not to have let ourselves be frightened out of our wits,” and bade one of them go over and search the house.
sentences.n: “sich ins Bockshorn jagen lassen” — literally ‘to let oneself be driven into a goat’s horn’ — is an idiom for being intimidated or scared into a corner. “hieß einen hingehen” = bade one (of them) go there; heißen + infinitive = to bid/order.

sentences.t: Der Abgeschickte fand alles still, gieng in die Küche, ein Licht anzuzünden, und weil er die glühenden, feurigen Augen der Katze für lebendige Kohlen ansah, hielt er ein Schwefelhölzchen daran, daß es Feuer fangen sollte.
sentences.l: The one sent found everything still, went into the kitchen to light a candle, and because he took the cat’s glowing, fiery eyes for live coals, he held a match to them, so that it should catch fire.
sentences.i: The messenger found everything quiet, went into the kitchen to light a candle, and because he took the cat’s glowing, fiery eyes for live coals, he held a match to them to make it catch fire.
sentences.n: “der Abgeschickte” = the one sent off (nominalized past participle). “etwas für (etwas) ansehen” = to take/mistake one thing for another. “Schwefelhölzchen” (sulphur-stick) is an old word for a match.

sentences.t: Aber die Katze verstand keinen Spaß, sprang ihm ins Gesicht, spie und kratzte.
sentences.l: But the cat understood no joke, sprang into his face, spat and scratched.
sentences.i: But the cat was in no mood for jokes, sprang at his face, spat and scratched.
sentences.n: “keinen Spaß verstehen” = to take no joke, be in no mood for jest. “spie” is the preterite of speien (to spit).

sentences.t: Da erschrack er gewaltig, lief und wollte zur Hinterthüre hinaus, aber der Hund, der da lag, sprang auf und biß ihn ins Bein: und als er über den Hof an dem Miste vorbei rannte, gab ihm der Esel noch einen tüchtigen Schlag mit dem Hinterfuß; der Hahn aber, der vom Lärmen aus dem Schlaf geweckt und munter geworden war, rief vom Balken herab „kikeriki!“
sentences.l: Then he took fright terribly, ran and wanted to get out at the back door, but the dog, who lay there, sprang up and bit him in the leg: and as he ran across the yard past the dung-heap, the donkey gave him a hearty blow with his hind foot; but the cock, who had been woken from sleep by the noise and had grown lively, called down from the beam, “cock-a-doodle-doo!”
sentences.i: Then he took a terrible fright, ran, and made for the back door, but the dog, who lay there, sprang up and bit him in the leg: and as he ran across the yard past the dung-heap, the donkey gave him a good hard kick with his hind foot; and the cock, roused from sleep by the din and grown wide awake, called down from the beam, “cock-a-doodle-doo!”
sentences.n: “erschrack” is the archaic strong preterite of erschrecken (to be frightened; modern erschrak). “vom Lärmen geweckt” = woken by the noise. “kikeriki” is the German for a cock’s crow.

sentences.t: Da lief der Räuber, was er konnte, zu seinem Hauptmann zurück und sprach „ach, in dem Haus sitzt eine gräuliche Hexe, die hat mich angehaucht und mit ihren langen Fingern mir das Gesicht zerkratzt: und vor der Thüre steht ein Mann mit einem Messer, der hat mich ins Bein gestochen: und auf dem Hof liegt ein schwarzes Ungethüm, das hat mit einer Holzkeule auf mich losgeschlagen: und oben auf dem Dache, da sitzt der Richter, der rief bringt mir den Schelm her.
sentences.l: Then the robber ran, as fast as he could, back to his captain and said, “oh, in the house there sits a hideous witch, who breathed on me and scratched my face with her long fingers: and before the door there stands a man with a knife, who stabbed me in the leg: and in the yard there lies a black monster, which struck out at me with a wooden club: and up on the roof, there sits the judge, who called ‘bring me the rogue here.’
sentences.i: Then the robber ran back to his captain as fast as he could and said, “oh, in the house there sits a hideous witch, who breathed on me and clawed my face with her long fingers: and before the door stands a man with a knife, who stabbed me in the leg: and in the yard lies a black monster, which laid into me with a wooden club: and up on the roof sits the judge, who cried, ‘bring me the rogue!’
sentences.n: The robber’s tale turns each animal into a monster: the cat is a witch (Hexe), the dog a knifeman, the donkey a black Ungethüm (monster), the cock a judge. “mir das Gesicht zerkratzt” uses the dative of possession. “Th-” spellings: Thüre, Ungethüm; “Dache” = Dach (roof).

sentences.t: Da machte ich daß ich fortkam.“
sentences.l: Then I saw to it that I got away.”
sentences.i: So I made off as fast as I could.”
sentences.n: “machen daß ...” (colloquial) = to see to it that / manage to; “daß ich fortkam” = that I got away.

sentences.t: Von nun an getrauten sich die Räuber nicht weiter in das Haus, den vier Bremer Musikanten gefiels aber so wohl darin, daß sie nicht wieder heraus wollten.
sentences.l: From now on the robbers did not dare go further into the house, but it pleased the four Bremen musicians so well within, that they no longer wanted to come out.
sentences.i: From then on the robbers dared not venture into the house again, but the four Bremen musicians liked it so well there that they had no wish ever to leave.
sentences.n: “sich getrauen” = to dare (reflexive). “gefiels” = gefiel es; “den vier ... Musikanten gefiel es” — dative + gefallen — literally ‘it pleased to the four musicians’.

sentences.t: Und der das zuletzt erzählt hat, dem ist der Mund noch warm.
sentences.l: And he who has told this last, his mouth is still warm.
sentences.i: And whoever told this last, his mouth is still warm.
sentences.n: A traditional closing formula: the teller has only just finished, so his mouth is ‘still warm’ from speaking — the tale is fresh.
